speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four. Survivor is Mrs. Marion Lazan, and the interviewer is Mark Goldberg. Just briefly, why don't you bring us from 1948 to the present and tell us a little bit about what happened to you in this country.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] As I-- I said, we arrived April 23, which was-- happened to be Erev Pesach. Actually, that night was Pesach, was the Seder. And we arrived in Hoboken, and our Aunt Klara, my father's sister who lived here since 1936, met us at-- in Hoboken at the-- the docks, and she took us to her home. And there we celebrated Passover with her. It was a-- a bittersweet reunion because her brother was not here anymore, but yet she was-- and we were, of course, grateful that at least our-- the three of us were intact and that we were able to enjoy celebrating the holiday with-- with relatives in freedom. We-- soon after Pesach, we [SIGHS] registered with the HIAS, the Hebrew Immigration Aid Society. And they placed us into a-- a hotel called Capitol Hall, I remember, on the west side. And we were in one room. And I think they gave us a few dollars to-- to buy food and things. And I remember that my brother Al's craving was fruit cocktail, so my mother bought him the biggest can of fruit cocktail that was available. I, on the other hand, liked potato chips and mayonnaise. And my mother bought me the biggest bag of potato chips and a big jar of mayonnaise. And we indulged. And of course, the-- my treats didn't agree with me nearly as much as my brother's fruit cocktail agreed with him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were here in-- in New York until about June, and then the HIAS [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] found a place for us in Peoria, Illinois. I never heard of Illinois, let alone Peoria. And there, supposedly, the Jewish community had a room for a family. When we arrived by train, we were brought into our quarters, and they were very meager quarters. There was one apartment and there were already two other families in the apartment. So it really was-- there really was not the room that we thought was available. And it was quite primitive, also. It was in the poor section of town. We had one icebox for the three families. It was a wooden icebox, and I remember two or three times a week the iceman came, and we got blocks of ice. It was all new experience for us, but-- and it was OK. I-- it was the summertime, so school had not-- was not in session. My brother and I, my mother, we were given private English lessons. I also was able to get a job at $0.15 an hour working in a laundry. My brother worked in a jewelry store doing odd jobs. I don't think he made more than $0.25 an hour. And my mother did housework, and that was because she couldn't do anything else because of the language barrier. And-- but we weren't proud. It was perfectly fine. As long as we were able to earn a few dollars, it didn't really matter what we did. We were thrilled and pleased that we had the freedom in-- in-- to work and to do. And we pooled our monies, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we-- I remember while we lived here-- that was on Munson Street in Peoria, Illinois. It was right in back of a synagogue. I remember the first Hanukkah, Al, my brother, gave me what he thought was a very special gift, a pair of nylons. I was 13 years old. I remember being most upset with that gift. I cried so bitterly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I know that my brother was disappointed, but I explained that I just really didn't want those nylons. I did not want to be grown up yet. I did not have a childhood. And I-- I felt that I-- I needed more time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the nylons were put away. But it didn't take long, and I put them on. And-- at that time also, I weighed 136 pounds. I put on 100 pounds in two and a half years' time, three years' time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] school started in September, and I was put in fourth grade at the age of 13, again, because I did not know the language. I was very good in geography, European geography. And I was pretty good in arithmetic, but that did not help me here in America. So not knowing the language, not being able to understand any of the instructions in school, they put me in the fourth grade. And there I was with many black students and other students. And I-- mind you, I had never seen-- I've only seen white people all my life. And I was still-- always a little taken aback, and yet they were my best friends. These children [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were younger than I am, much younger, and I didn't expect them to understand what I went through. I didn't speak their language. I maybe even looked different than they expected, and I spoke different, and I-- I acted different. And here they were helping me, helping me the best that they could, and more. Helped me with the homework. I went over to their house. They came to my house. They were wonderful. It is just amazing how good people and children can be to one another. I know it can be otherwise, too, but in my case, it was a wonderful experience. And they helped me along, and along with [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] extra help in school-- I went to summer school. I stayed after school. I also worked. Eventually I caught up in school, and I was able to graduate high school at the age of 18 along with all my other friends. And I made some very dear friends in Peoria. And I graduated eighth out of 265 students. But that was really not because of any intelligence or of any-- anything special. It was just the will to show that I could do it. I could do it as well if not better than the rest of them, and the rest of my-- my friends. I also worked, continuously worked. And I worked in a department store and sold dresses, $1.57 one dress, two for $3.00. And this was at the Szold's department store. The Szold's department store was owned by Henrietta Szold's two nephews. Henrietta Szold is the founder of the Hadassah organization. And it's very interesting because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was a past president of Hadassah here in New York, and my sister-in-law was a past chapter president of Hadassah. So it was all-- everything sort of ties in together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We pooled our monies, my brother, my mother, and I. And it really did not take very long, and we were able to move out of our quarters in-- on Munson Street onto Third-- to an apartment of our own. And it felt mighty good to have our own place. We-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] At the age-- in October of 1951, at the age of 16, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- I met my husband, Nathaniel. And this was in a very unusual, wonderful way. It was on Yom Kippur, in synagogue, that we met. My husband Nathaniel was a student at that time at Bradley University in Peoria, Illinois. And he was seated with the students upstairs at the synagogue. It was an Orthodox congregation, so the women were separated from the men. The women sat upstairs and the students were sitting in back of the-- the women. And I guess he spotted me, and at the end of services, he asked to walk-- if he could walk me home. And that was the beginning of a wonderful, wonderful relationship. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we became engaged on-- in-- in March of '52, and we were married in August 2, 1953, in New York. And as I had said earlier, we're the proud parents of three lovely children-- intelligent, sensitive, wonderful children-- and the proud grandparents of five little ones. And that is pretty much the story of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you have had an extraordinary life. And if we look back 50 years, you had come through Westerbork, and you were in Bergen-Belsen, coming to the end of your time at Bergen-Belsen, and it was a very difficult time. And then the aftermath of the war. And here it is 50 years later, and you're in a beautiful home in a lovely community. You have a-- a marvelous family. Your life is completely different. What do you make of all this? What kind of reflection do you have on this? Or is there anything you want to say about this enormous gap in your experience between where you were 50 years ago and where you were today? As I had said earlier, there are very, very mixed feelings within me. I-- I-- I-- I do have feelings of-- of anxiety and of anger, and why should this have had happened? And-- and-- all of these feelings are within me that are tremendous turmoil. And I am obviously tremendously grateful that-- that we came out of this, and-- and with a positive, good outlook on life.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that there are many survivors or people who came out and who were bitter and angry, and rightfully so. I'm just very grateful that-- that I came out healthy in mind and with a positive good outlook. And again, I-- I attribute much of it, or all of it, to-- to my mother and to my husband, who has always been understanding and-- and loving and caring. And I'm-- I'm forever grateful that I have-- have him. He is a very unusual human being. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it-- it is-- it is just wonderful to-- to be able to-- to tell the story, and and it must be told. There is just no doubt that in a few years' time there won't be too many of us left to tell it. And it has to be told and retold over and over again. And-- and when I speak to students or in organizations, I ask only one thing, to please retell my story to their family, to their friends. And ask them, in turn, to tell it to their friends and their families. It has to be retold. It has to be repeated over and over again. And it to be taught in schools. And it has to become a part of everyone's lives, as difficult as it is. Only then can we guard it against it ever happening again. It has to be taught in schools, and it has to be repeated. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I would like to share, if I may, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a letter that was written by my niece, Mona. Mona is a very dear person, and she is married to [? Stephen ?] Elsen. [? Stephen ?] is the son of my sister-in-law, Nathaniel's sister Naomi. And in June of 1991, I get this letter in response to-- I sent her my story in-- in writing. And this is the letter that she wrote to me. It says, 'Dear Aunt Marion, we want to thank you and Uncle Nat for sharing your interview with us. We certainly knew you'd been through the camps as a child, and we knew what that meant in a vague sort of way, as much as any of us who haven't experienced it can know. But reading your story brought it home to us in a much more imminent way. I admire your positive approach to life more than you can know. Steve and I spent a lot of hours debating our coming child. Our reasons for wanting another are varied, but in my mind, wanting this one was in some way tied in to all those children who did not survive. We always knew we'd try to have a family of two children, but this one is extra, and in a very tiny way, is our means of saying, Hitler did not win. We hope we can raise all of our-- all our children to have an appreciation of what came before them.' [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was overwhelmed by this heartwarming, sensitive, beautiful letter written. And Anna Ida Elsen was born August 22, 1991. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She is-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she's the younger sister of nine-year-old Elana [POINTING] and six-year-old Howie. This is Mona and [? Stephen ?] and her-- their children.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. These are my grandparents, my father's parents, Seline and Max Blumenthal, which this picture was taken approximately 19-- in the 1920s in Hoya, where we lived. And this is the house in which we lived, with the [POINTS] apartments up above. I believe we lived on the upper floor [POINTING] and the grandparents lived on the second floor. And then this was our store, a shoe store and clothing store. Here's the house again. But this one has the family standing in front of it, the grandparents, and my father's sisters Klara and Rosie, and my father Walter. And here are my grandparents, my mother's parents. It was their wedding picture, and taken approximately 1906 in northern Germany. I think it was in in Tilsit or one of those areas in-- in northern East Prussia. And [POINTS] here is a picture of my parents shortly after they were married in 1931, also in Hoya, Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you tell us what the hanger and the other things are? Why don't you hold them up so we can--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's pretty amazing that we were able to save these three items. These are all from the business, from the shoe business in Hoya. They were saved, we're sure, by my Aunt Rosie, who was in hiding. And she gave them to us after a while. This is a shoe horn from the store. And this is a matchbox, which was either given out as advertising, or whatever. It's-- I-- I really don't know. And this is a hanger that was actually used in the store, with the name, as you can see, R. Feist, Hoya. And the Weser. Weser was the name of the river that was near Hoya. And it says Schuhe and Herrenkleidung, shoes and men's clothing. And these are the mementos of our business. These three items are mementos from-- mementos from the store that we had in-- in Hoya, and they were saved by my aunt-- Auntie Rosie, my father's sister, who was in hiding in Holland. This is a shoe horn and a matchbox. And this is a hanger that was actually used in the store. And it says R. Feist, Hoya, and the Weser, which is the name of the river. And it says Schuhe und Herrenkleidung, shoes and-- and men's clothing. My brother's and my birth certificates. This is Al's birth certificate and this is mine. What is interesting in this one is that in the early '30s, they added-- to the original birth certificates, they added [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the name Israel for a men's-- on a men's birth certificate and the name Sara, to a woman's, female's birth certificate. And my brother had been in Hoya and been back to Germany about a year and a half ago. And there he was-- went to the mayor, to the-- and he was able to get his birth certificate, and in it he saw that they had written this-- the name Israel. And then in-- after the war, they deleted the name Israel. And it was just interesting to note that fact. And with mine, they didn't delete it yet. But I-- I'll hold onto the name Sara. It's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I want to get an overshot. Please bring the [? setup. ?] Straight. Straight.

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken in Holland in 1938 of myself and the friends that I made in Holland. I was, at that time, four years old, and I was staying with my Aunt Rosie, my father's sister, who was married to a Dutch person and lived in Holland in [INAUDIBLE]. And the reason I was with her at that time is because my parents had moved from Hoya to Hanover. And in the interim, it was easier for me to be out of the way and to be in Holland. This is a picture of my brother Al and myself in 1936, and a picture of my family, my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please, about these two.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my brother and myself in 1936, and my parents and brother and I, also in the '30s. And below are a picture of my brother and myself. And this is a passport picture of mine when we left Germany, I guess, if we needed that, or some sort of a ID. These are pictures taken in [INAUDIBLE], where my parents were responsible for taking care of some 125 children. And [POINTING] this is in Westerbork, my father, mother, my brother Al, and myself. [POINTING] Down below is also Westerbork, where a group of children were together. And here is a picture of myself, my brother Al and I. This was a surprise picture taken for my mother's birthday. And here is a picture of-- taken-- these were taken 1943-- of my mother [POINTS] and a group of people taking English lessons. My father always took pride in learning English. His aim and his feelings and his thoughts were always with-- with immigrating and coming to America. And when he comes to America, he has to know English. And he always had a dictionary by him. And it was very important for him to [POINTS] learn the language the best he could. And here's a picture of-- one of them is my mother, and one of them my father's group. This, of course, is the star [POINTS] that we were forced to wear at all times on outer clothing. And my father worked in a shoe [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shop, and this is a picture of him and my brother and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: All these pictures are in are in Westerbork, is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. These are all--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. These were all taken in Westerbork. These were taken in Westerbork, as well as these on these two pages. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And there's my father. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please point [? it out ?] [? again. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my mother in the group of [POINTS] people who were learning English. And this is in Westerbork in 1942. And here's a picture of my father with his group. He may have been a bit more advanced than my mother. Therefore, they were in separate groups. And this was all taken in Westerbork, 1942, '43. And these pictures, I think, were taken in 1941. Later on in Westerbork, no more pictures were allowed to be taken. OK. This was actually-- in-- as I-- I can remember the very first picture that was taken of me after we were liberated. It was taken 1945 in Amsterdam. My hair was just growing in slightly. And this is [? Brissom ?], [POINTS] group of our-- of the children who were there with me. Here is [POINTING] Al, my brother, and here am I. And this is my dearest friend Suzy [INAUDIBLE] Birnbaum, [INAUDIBLE] who lives now in-- in Yerushalayim, in Jerusalem, with her five children and grandchildren. And this is my [POINTS] brother, mother, and I in her apartment in Amsterdam in 1947. These were all taken in 1947 of [POINTING] my mother, my brother, and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here is my brother in Holland on-- Your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my brother in his knickers. [POINTS] He was very proud of his knickers. Taken in-- in 1946 in Amsterdam, and it's on-- right in front of the palace, the Royal Palace in Amsterdam. And there's my brother and myself in 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much of anything. It's my brother and I sitting by a window, also in 1947 in Amsterdam. Must have been my mother's apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: With a tulip [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Is there a tulip in there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.
